segments.speaker: designer
segments.label: Designer
segments.text:
# Sigma-1 Design Recommendations

Let me work through each decision point for the Sigma-1 website — a lighting and visual production company serving event professionals, with a 533-product equipment catalog, self-service quoting, and an embedded AI chat agent (Morgan).

**Key context I'm designing around:**
- The existing site (sigma-1.com) is a minimal, dark-themed landing page — mostly a contact form with dramatic lighting imagery
- The PRD specifies Next.js 15, React 19, shadcn/ui, TailwindCSS 4, and Effect
- Primary users are event producers and production managers browsing equipment, checking availability, and requesting quotes
- The Morgan chat widget must coexist with the main site visually
- Stitch has generated both desktop and mobile modernization candidates

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-11
category: ux-behavior
question: What is the primary navigation paradigm for the Sigma-1 website: sidebar, topbar, or mobile-first bottom navigation?
recommendation: B

OPTION_A:
label: "Sidebar Navigation (Collapsible)"
description: Persistent left sidebar on desktop with category tree for equipment browsing, collapsing to a hamburger drawer on mobile. Think Stripe Docs or Linear — always-visible navigation with deep hierarchy support.
visual_reference: Stitch desktop candidate (stitch-web-1) — could be adapted to include a sidebar panel for equipment categories alongside the main content area.
strengths: Excellent for deep catalog browsing across 24 equipment categories. Keeps navigation visible while users explore products. Natural fit for filter/facet patterns alongside product grids. Scales well if admin-facing pages are ever exposed here.
tradeoffs: Consumes horizontal real estate on a site that benefits from full-width hero imagery and portfolio galleries. Feels like an application rather than a marketing/catalog site — may intimidate event planners who just want to browse and inquire. The existing sigma-1.com brand is theatrical and full-bleed; a sidebar fights that aesthetic.

OPTION_B:
label: "Sticky Topbar with Category Mega-Menu"
description: Compact sticky header with logo, primary nav links (Equipment, Portfolio, Quote, Contact), and a mega-menu dropdown for equipment categories. On mobile, this collapses to a hamburger with full-screen overlay. Morgan chat widget floats as a bottom-right FAB.
visual_reference: Stitch desktop candidate (stitch-web-1) shows a clean top-aligned layout with clear hierarchy. The mobile candidate (stitch-mobile-2) shows a hamburger-driven pattern. Reference: vercel.com/marketplace or similar catalog sites with topbar + mega-menu.
strengths: Preserves full-width layout for hero imagery, portfolio galleries, and dramatic visual storytelling that matches the Perception Events brand. Mega-menu gives one-click access to all 24 categories without leaving context. Universally understood pattern — zero learning curve for event production clients. Clean coexistence with the Morgan chat FAB (no spatial conflict). Matches the Stitch-generated candidates naturally.
tradeoffs: Mega-menus require careful implementation for keyboard accessibility (arrow-key navigation, escape-to-close). 24 categories need thoughtful grouping to avoid overwhelming the dropdown — likely needs 3-4 category groups. Less persistent than a sidebar for deep catalog browsing sessions.

OPTION_C:
label: "Mobile-First Bottom Tab Bar (Adaptive)"
description: Bottom tab navigation (Equipment, Quote, Portfolio, Chat) as the primary paradigm on all viewports, adapting to a fixed bottom bar on desktop as well (à la some PWA-style apps). Content area fills the remaining space above.
visual_reference: Stitch mobile candidate (stitch-mobile-2) could accommodate this. Think of the Expo mobile app pattern referenced in the PRD architecture.
strengths: Thumb-friendly on mobile. Gives Morgan chat a first-class tab rather than a floating widget. Consistent navigation between web and the eventual Expo mobile app.
tradeoffs: Bottom bars on desktop feel foreign and waste valuable vertical space on large screens. Goes against web convention — event planners arriving from Google expect a topbar on a website. The 533-product catalog needs more navigation depth than 4-5 tabs can provide. Would require significant deviation from Stitch candidates. The web and mobile app should have appropriate-to-platform navigation, not forced parity.

REASONING:
The Sigma-1 website is a public-facing catalog and lead-generation tool, not an internal application. Event production clients arriving from Google or Signal referrals need a conventional, confidence-building web experience. The sticky topbar with mega-menu preserves the dramatic full-bleed imagery that defines the Perception Events brand while giving structured access to 24 equipment categories. It aligns naturally with both Stitch-generated candidates and the shadcn/ui navigation components. The Morgan chat widget lives as a floating FAB — spatially separate from navigation, always accessible but never competing for the same screen edge.
```

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-12
category: component-library
question: Which data table component library should be used for the equipment catalog and quote builder in the website?
recommendation: B

OPTION_A:
label: "shadcn/ui Table Components Only"
description: Use shadcn/ui's built-in Table, TableHeader, TableBody, TableRow, and TableCell primitives for all tabular display. Implement sorting, filtering, and pagination manually using React state and Effect for data fetching.
visual_reference: shadcn/ui table examples at ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/data-table — clean, minimal styling that matches the design system.
strengths: Zero additional dependencies. Full visual consistency with the rest of the shadcn/ui component set. Complete control over markup and styling. Smallest bundle size.
tradeoffs: shadcn/ui's table docs already recommend TanStack Table under the hood for anything beyond static display. Building sorting, column resizing, row selection, and virtualization from scratch is significant engineering effort for a 533-product catalog. No built-in support for column pinning or expandable rows (useful for product specs). You'd end up rebuilding TanStack Table poorly.

OPTION_B:
label: "TanStack Table + shadcn/ui Styling"
description: Use TanStack Table (v8) as the headless logic layer for sorting, filtering, pagination, column visibility, and row selection. Render using shadcn/ui Table primitives for consistent styling. This is the pattern shadcn/ui itself documents as "Data Table."
visual_reference: shadcn/ui Data Table example (ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/data-table) — this IS the TanStack Table + shadcn pattern. Reference the Stitch desktop candidate for layout context.
strengths: Battle-tested headless table logic with zero opinion on styling — pairs perfectly with shadcn/ui primitives. Supports all features the catalog needs: multi-column sorting, faceted filtering (by category, price range, availability), pagination for 533+ products, column visibility toggles, and row selection for the quote builder. TypeScript-first API integrates cleanly with Effect Schema for type-safe column definitions. ~12KB gzipped — minimal cost. The shadcn/ui documentation literally uses this approach.
tradeoffs: One additional dependency (though it's the official shadcn recommendation). Requires understanding TanStack Table's headless API — the learning curve is moderate but well-documented. Column definitions are verbose compared to declarative alternatives.

OPTION_C:
label: "Custom Table with Effect Integration"
description: Build a bespoke table component that uses Effect streams for data fetching, Effect Schema for column type definitions, and Effect's fiber model for background filtering/sorting. Render with shadcn/ui primitives.
visual_reference: No existing reference — this would be a novel implementation.
strengths: Maximum type safety end-to-end from API response through rendering. Could leverage Effect's streaming for real-time availability updates in table cells. Architecturally elegant for a team deeply invested in Effect.
tradeoffs: Significant engineering time building what TanStack Table already provides. No community examples or documentation to reference. Effect's value in this stack is for data fetching and validation, not UI rendering logic — this conflates concerns. Risk of building something brittle that only one developer understands. The PRD targets shipping speed; this is a research project.

REASONING:
The shadcn/ui documentation explicitly recommends TanStack Table for data tables — it's not an alternative to shadcn, it's the blessed approach. With 533 products across 24 categories, the catalog needs faceted filtering, sortable columns, and pagination at minimum. The quote builder adds row selection and quantity editing. TanStack Table provides all of this headlessly while shadcn/ui primitives handle the visual layer. Effect integrates at the data-fetching boundary (TanStack Query + Effect), not inside the table rendering logic. This is the clear winner — the other options are either insufficient or over-engineered.
```

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-13
category: design-system
question: How should theming and design tokens be managed across the website and web chat widget?
recommendation: A

OPTION_A:
label: "TailwindCSS 4 + Extended shadcn/ui Tokens"
description: Use shadcn/ui's built-in CSS custom property token system (--background, --foreground, --primary, --muted, etc.) as the foundation. Extend it with Sigma-1 brand tokens (--sigma-accent, --sigma-surface-glow) in the Tailwind 4 theme configuration. The Morgan chat widget inherits the same token set via a scoped CSS class (.morgan-widget) that can override specific values.
visual_reference: Stitch desktop candidate (stitch-web-1) shows a dark-themed layout consistent with the existing sigma-1.com aesthetic. The token system would define both dark (default, matching brand) and light modes.
strengths: shadcn/ui already ships a mature token architecture with dark/light mode support — no need to invent one. TailwindCSS 4's native CSS-first configuration (@theme directive) makes extending tokens trivial without extra build tooling. The chat widget inherits brand tokens automatically but can scope overrides for readability (e.g., lighter backgrounds in the chat panel for conversation legibility). One source of truth: tokens defined in CSS, consumed by Tailwind utilities, referenced by shadcn components. Fastest path to a cohesive look across site + widget.
tradeoffs: Token naming is coupled to shadcn/ui's conventions (--primary, --secondary, --accent). If you later need tokens that don't map to shadcn's semantic model, you extend rather than replace. Tailwind 4's @theme is newer — some shadcn/ui examples still reference Tailwind 3 config syntax, so you'll need to translate.

OPTION_B:
label: "Custom Token Layer on TailwindCSS"
description: Define a standalone design token specification (JSON or YAML) following the DTCG (Design Tokens Community Group) format. Generate CSS custom properties and Tailwind theme extensions from this spec using a build step (Style Dictionary or tokens-studio). shadcn/ui components consume these generated tokens.
visual_reference: Same visual output as Option A, but with an additional abstraction layer between token definition and consumption.
strengths: Platform-agnostic token definitions that could feed into the Expo mobile app, email templates, or Figma plugins. Industry-standard format (DTCG) makes future tooling integration easier. Clean separation between design decisions (tokens) and implementation (Tailwind/CSS).
tradeoffs: Adds a build step and a tool dependency (Style Dictionary or similar) to the frontend pipeline. For a v1 website + chat widget, this is premature abstraction — the Expo app is a separate target and can consume the same CSS custom properties or define its own platform-appropriate tokens. Slower iteration during development: change a token → run build → see result, vs. change a CSS variable → see result immediately.

OPTION_C:
label: "CSS Custom Properties + Headless Components"
description: Bypass both Tailwind's theme system and shadcn/ui's token conventions. Define all design tokens as raw CSS custom properties in a global stylesheet. Use Radix UI primitives directly (headless, unstyled) and apply tokens via vanilla CSS or CSS Modules. Maximum flexibility, zero framework coupling.
visual_reference: No specific reference — this produces a fully custom visual language not tied to any component library's defaults.
strengths: Total control over every token name, value, and scope. No coupling to shadcn or Tailwind conventions. Could produce the most distinctive visual identity since nothing defaults to a library's look.
tradeoffs: Abandons the PRD's specified stack (shadcn/ui + TailwindCSS 4). Requires building or restyling every component from scratch — buttons, dialogs, dropdowns, forms, tables — all the things shadcn/ui provides ready-made. For a 533-product catalog with quote builder, availability checker, and chat widget, this is months of UI work that doesn't exist with Option A. The brand distinctiveness gain doesn't justify the cost at v1.

REASONING:
The PRD explicitly specifies shadcn/ui + TailwindCSS 4, and that's the right call. shadcn/ui's token system is mature, supports dark/light modes out of the box, and maps cleanly to the dark, dramatic aesthetic that Perception Events already uses (visible in both sigma-1.com and the Stitch candidates). Extending these tokens for brand-specific values (glow effects, accent colors for lighting categories) is a 30-minute job in Tailwind 4's @theme directive. The Morgan chat widget gets scoped overrides via a CSS class — same tokens, adjusted for conversational UI legibility. Option B's token build pipeline adds value when you have 3+ platforms consuming the same tokens; at v1 with web + embedded widget, it's overhead without payoff.
```

---

## Verification Checklist

- [x] Every design decision point from the input is addressed (dp-11, dp-12, dp-13)
- [x] Each option has label, description, strengths, and tradeoffs
- [x] A recommendation is stated for every decision (B, B, A)
- [x] Reasoning references the PRD, user needs, or project constraints
- [x] No option is a strawman — all three options per decision are defensible choices
- [x] Visual references include Stitch candidates where relevant (stitch-web-1, stitch-mobile-2)
